blocks.content: He took her on a Saturday in November when the sky was the flat white of late autumn and the fields had gone brown and still. He told himself it was just a view. He told himself it was just a thing he'd said he would do.

The water tower hadn't changed. It never changed — the same rust-ringed ladder, the same catwalk at the top, the same view of Millhaven spread out below like something out of a model-train set. He'd been up here four times in his life. Three of them with Daisy.

blocks.speaker: Brooke
blocks.content: Okay. This is worth the climb.

blocks.speaker: Brooke
blocks.content: You can see the whole town.

He pointed it out. She leaned over the railing to look and he noticed the familiar drop below, the way the wind moved up here, the quality of the silence. He had stood in this exact spot and looked at this exact view and said things out loud that he hadn't meant to say. He remembered the particular shade of sky that evening. He remembered the way Daisy had looked at the town below them with her hair doing the thing it did when the wind caught it.
He didn't think about that either.

blocks.speaker: Brooke
blocks.content: I get why you like it up here.

blocks.speaker: Brooke
blocks.content: Is that the field? Behind those houses?
She turned to look at him instead of the view, and the catwalk was narrow and she was close, and he already knew what was coming and he didn't move.
